clean_story: Bright petals.  Silent survival tools. "Flower" comes from the Old French flor, meaning "blossom"—a plant’s colorful advertisement for a hidden transaction. We usually see flowers as decoration, but they’re more like a bee’s grocery store sign. Petals aren’t just pretty—they’re solar panels, heaters, and landing pads. Take the Arctic poppy:Its bowl-shaped petals act like a satellite dish, focusing sunlight to warm its center by 5–10°C, luring cold-resistant insects. Or the corpse flower—its rotting-meat stench mimics a dead animal, tricking flies into pollinating it. Some, like the bird-of-paradise, use structural color—microscopic ridges on petals that bend light into iridescent blues, invisible to humans but a beacon for birds. Others, like the evening primrose, open only at dusk, their white petals glowing under moonlight to guide moths. Flowers even manipulate:The orchid Ophrys apifera mimics a female wasp’s shape and scent so precisely that males try to mate with it, accidentally carrying pollen. No wasted energy—every color, smell, or curve serves one goal: reproduction without movement. Three related subjects are plant deception, pollinator vision and flower fossils.
